question: How is the reactor coolant circulation conducted in the NuScale NPM?

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: This section describes the evaluation of the NuScale Power Plant's system performance, focusing on optimizing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressure to maximize electrical generation and maintain safety margins. Calculations using thermal hydraulic software and hand verification account for uncertainties and ensure the natural circulation design is effective, with low pressure losses and robust performance across different operating conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe and efficient cooling, maintain water chemistry, and provide uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, thereby protecting fuel integrity during normal and off-normal events.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to prevent fuel design limit violations during all operational conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various system components and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing continuous monitoring and control.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the Reactor Coolant System’s DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It specifies that DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, with additional testing for valve position and actuator power, and includes a one-time in-situ test of the DHRS function during initial startup of the first reactor module.

edges.description: Section B describes the design basis that the NuScale NPM relies on natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section A evaluates system performance based on these established design parameters.

Section B provides a general overview stating that the NuScale NPM uses natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section A discusses how system parameters are optimized within this natural circulation framework to achieve optimal performance.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system performance parameters for reactor coolant circulation, while Section B details specific operational steps and component actions (such as isolation of SGs and pressure adjustments) that implement and affect the coolant circulation described in Section A.

Section A describes the overall design principle of the NuScale NPM reactor coolant system relying on natural circulation, while Section B details specific operational steps and component actions (such as isolation of steam generators and pressurizer water level adjustments) that implement and support this circulation method.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation, while Section B details specific operational steps and components involved in managing coolant and pressure within that system, thus specifying how the general process described in A is implemented.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining pressure equilibrium within the reactor coolant system, while Section B discusses the evaluation of system parameters (including coolant circulation) to optimize plant performance, connecting them through the operational requirements and design considerations for effective coolant circulation.

Section A describes the pressurizer as an integral component of the reactor vessel that interacts with the naturally circulating reactor coolant, while Section B provides the overall design basis stating that the NuScale NPM relies on natural circulation for reactor coolant flow and includes the pressurizer as part of the RCS.

Section A describes the pressurizer as an integral component of the reactor vessel that interacts with the naturally circulating reactor coolant, while Section B provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system, specifying that it relies on natural circulation and includes the pressurizer as a subsystem component.

Section A details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B explains the functional connection of the DHRS to the steam and feed piping for decay heat removal, linking the design and operational aspects of the reactor coolant circulation.

Section B describes the fundamental method of reactor coolant circulation (natural circulation without pumps) in the NuScale NPM, while Section A details inspection and testing requirements for components (such as DHRS valves) that are part of the systems supporting or connected to the reactor coolant system.

Section B describes the overall method of reactor coolant circulation in the NuScale NPM (natural circulation without pumps), while Section A details inspection and classification requirements for components (such as valves) within the systems that support or interact with this circulation, thus providing specific implementation details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining pressure and facilitating coolant flow within the reactor vessel, while Section B outlines inspection requirements for high-point vents and related components, both of which are integral to ensuring proper reactor coolant circulation and system integrity.
